or **sister channels** are [radio](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") or [television stations](/wiki/Television_station "Television station") operated by the same company, either by [direct ownership](/wiki/Duopoly_(broadcasting) "Duopoly (broadcasting)") or through a [management agreement](/wiki/Local_marketing_agreement "Local marketing agreement").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

or **sister channels**, in many cases, are cable or satellite channels which are launched to either broadcast series which either premiered on the main network but has been moved out of the higher-priority schedule (such as [TV Land](/wiki/TV_Land "TV Land") or  [Boomerang](/wiki/Boomerang_(TV_Channel) "Boomerang (TV Channel)")), fulfill a specific niche of content which would not be fulfilled on the main network (such as the [Nick Jr. Channel](/wiki/Nick_Jr._Channel "Nick Jr. Channel")[[2]](#cite_note-2) or [Nicktoons](/wiki/Nicktoons_(American_TV_channel) "Nicktoons (American TV channel)")) or broadcast to a wider audience than the main network (such as [CNN International](/wiki/CNN_International "CNN International") or [Al Jazeera English](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English "Al Jazeera English")). However, in other cases, these cable or satellite channels may only share common ownership.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Broadcast stations of the same company

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Sister_station "Special:EditPage/Sister station") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Sister station"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Sister+station%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Sister+station%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Sister+station%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Sister+station%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Sister+station%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Sister+station%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting"), **sister stations** or **sister channels** are [radio](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station") or [television stations](/wiki/Television_station "Television station") operated by the same company, either by [direct ownership](/wiki/Duopoly_(broadcasting) "Duopoly (broadcasting)") or through a [management agreement](/wiki/Local_marketing_agreement "Local marketing agreement").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Radio sister stations will often have different [formats](/wiki/Radio_format "Radio format"), and sometimes one station is on the [AM band](/wiki/Amplitude_modulation "Amplitude modulation") while another is on the [FM band](/wiki/Frequency_modulation "Frequency modulation"). Conversely, several types of sister-station relationships exist in television; stations in the same city will usually be [affiliated](/wiki/Network_affiliate "Network affiliate") with different [television networks](/wiki/Television_network "Television network") (often one with a major network and the other with a secondary network), and may occasionally shift [television programs](/wiki/Television_program "Television program") between each other when local events require one station to interrupt its network feed.

Sister stations in separate (but often nearby) cities owned by the same company may or may not share a network affiliation. For example, [WNYW](/wiki/WNYW "WNYW") and [WWOR-TV](/wiki/WWOR-TV "WWOR-TV"), in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Secaucus, New Jersey](/wiki/Secaucus,_New_Jersey "Secaucus, New Jersey"), are both owned by [Fox Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Corporation "Fox Corporation"). WNYW is a Fox [owned-and-operated station](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_station "Owned-and-operated station"); WWOR-TV is a MyNetworkTV owned-and-operated station. [WPSG](/wiki/WPSG "WPSG") in Philadelphia and [KBCW](/wiki/KPYX "KPYX") in San Francisco were the flagship stations of [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") until 2023 and are owned by [CBS Television Stations](/wiki/CBS_Television_Stations "CBS Television Stations"). In addition, stations in different cities affiliated with the same network, but not sharing an ownership tie, may refer to each other informally as sister stations.

**Sister networks** or **sister channels**, in many cases, are cable or satellite channels which are launched to either broadcast series which either premiered on the main network but has been moved out of the higher-priority schedule (such as [TV Land](/wiki/TV_Land "TV Land") or  [Boomerang](/wiki/Boomerang_(TV_Channel) "Boomerang (TV Channel)")), fulfill a specific niche of content which would not be fulfilled on the main network (such as the [Nick Jr. Channel](/wiki/Nick_Jr._Channel "Nick Jr. Channel")[[2]](#cite_note-2) or [Nicktoons](/wiki/Nicktoons_(American_TV_channel) "Nicktoons (American TV channel)")) or broadcast to a wider audience than the main network (such as [CNN International](/wiki/CNN_International "CNN International") or [Al Jazeera English](/wiki/Al_Jazeera_English "Al Jazeera English")). However, in other cases, these cable or satellite channels may only share common ownership.

The establishment and proliferation of sister networks on cable, satellite and internet providers has become easier and more commercially profitable over the history of such media venues.
